title: Influence of strain on the anomalous Hall and Nernst effects in Fe thin films
authors: Ao Nakagawa, Ryo Toyama, Keisuke Masuda, Weinan Zhou, Hirofumi Suto, Kodchakorn Simalaotao, Yoshio Miura, Yuya Sakuraba, Tetsunori Koda
abstract: The anomalous Hall effect (AHE) and anomalous Nernst effect (ANE) are the transverse transport phenomena in magnetic materials, which reflect the Berry curvature arising from the electronic structure near the Fermi level. Lattice strain provides a direct means to tune these effects by modifying the electronic structure; however, disentangling the strain-induced effect through the Berry curvature modulations in multicomponent materials is challenging due to complexities arising from extrinsic contributions by impurities and disorder, as well as difficulties in simple direct comparison with first-principles calculations. In this study, we focus on Fe, a prototypical single element ferromagnet with a well-established electronic structure, and tune the sign and magnitude of the strain in epitaxial thin films of by varying the substrates and deposition conditions to investigate the strain effect on the AHE and ANE. Scaling law analysis revealed that the intrinsic anomalous Hall conductivity (AHC) exhibits a clear tetragonal distortion (c/a) dependence, in good agreement with theoretical calculations based on Berry curvature modification. In contrast, the anomalous Nernst conductivity (ANC) shows a pronounced deviation from the theoretical values and markedly different c/a dependence. These results demonstrate a crucial difference in the physical origin between the AHC and the ANC in the Fe films; the AHC is predominantly governed by intrinsic mechanisms, whereas the ANC is strongly influenced by the extrinsic contribution.
summary: This paper investigates how epitaxial strain affects the anomalous Hall and Nernst effects in iron thin films. By comparing films with different c/a ratios to theoretical Berry curvature calculations, the authors show that the Hall effect is primarily driven by intrinsic electronic structure changes. However, they find that the Nernst effect deviates significantly from theory due to strong extrinsic contributions. This distinction highlights the complexity of using strain to tune transverse transport in magnetic materials.
main_problem: The study aims to disentangle whether the strain-induced changes in the anomalous Hall effect (AHE) and anomalous Nernst effect (ANE) in Fe thin films are driven by intrinsic Berry curvature modulation or extrinsic scattering mechanisms.
main_result: The AHE is found to be predominantly governed by intrinsic mechanisms that follow theoretical predictions of Berry curvature modulation, whereas the ANE is strongly influenced by extrinsic contributions, leading to a significant deviation from theoretical predictions.
method: The researchers used epitaxial Fe thin films on MgO and MAO substrates to vary lattice strain, performed scaling law analysis on magnetotransport data, and compared experimental results with first-principles DFT calculations.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of 20 nm thick (001)-oriented epitaxial Fe thin films grown on MgO (tensile strain) and MgAl2O4 (compressive strain) substrates, covering a range of c/a ratios from 0.9825 to 1.0108.
key_observables: Anomalous Hall conductivity (AHC), anomalous Nernst conductivity (ANC), longitudinal resistivity, Hall resistivity, Seebeck coefficient, and c/a ratio.
important_parameters: Lattice strain (tensile vs. compressive), tetragonal distortion (c/a ratio), temperature (15 K to 300 K), and magnetic field.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that using a single-element ferromagnet like Fe minimizes complexities from multicomponent stoichiometry, though it notes that impurities from different sputtering systems can affect the skew scattering sign.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows XRD profiles demonstrating epitaxial growth and strain-induced lattice constant shifts; Figure 2 presents scaling analysis for separating AHE components; Figure 3 shows Nernst electric field and coefficients as a function of c/a ratio; Figure 4 compares experimental c/a dependence with theoretical energy dependence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of strain-induced effects, describes the experimental preparation of Fe films, presents structural characterization via XRD, details the magnetotransport and scaling analysis, compares results to DFT calculations, and concludes with a discussion on the differing physical origins of AHE and ANE.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
